Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent this semester. I'd set aside time to study, usually around 19 hours a week, but I'd often get distracted or feel too anxious to really focus. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to organize my notes with diagrams and color-coding, but the anxiety made it hard to stick to any real schedule. I had good intentions but poor follow-through most of the time.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I'd start the week thinking I'd study at set times, but then my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid the material entirely. When deadlines approached, I'd cram everything into a few stressful sessions. It wasn't healthy, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle even though I knew what I should be doing.
Q3: Usually what got me to log into the LMS was either a deadline notification or just guilt about not checking in for a while. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I was missing, which honestly made my anxiety worse. I'd see all the discussion posts and videos I hadn't watched and feel overwhelmed, so I'd close it again pretty quickly.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more routine, especially early in the semester when I was more optimistic. But that didn't last long. Once I started falling behind and my attendance dropped, the LMS became more of a source of stress than a helpful tool. I never really got into a sustainable habit with it.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I watched a decent amount of video content when I could focus. I also found the discussion posts somewhat helpful for understanding other perspectives, even though I didn't post as much as I should have. Visual materials like charts and diagrams in the course content really helped concepts click for me.
Q6: I definitely ignored or delayed the reading materials and longer text-based resources. They just didn't work with how I learn, and when my anxiety was high, staring at walls of text felt impossible. I also avoided some of the more complex assignments until the last minute because they felt too overwhelming to start.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate relevance or when it was presented visually. If I could watch something and understand it quickly, that felt productive. But if something required a lot of effort with unclear payoff, my motivation just tanked. I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just spinning my wheels.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they existed in a different universe for me. With required work already overwhelming me, optional stuff just got pushed aside completely. I'd tell myself I'd get to them once I caught up on the mandatory work, but that never happened. Required activities at least had the pressure of deadlines to motivate me.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd download PDFs and videos to study offline, especially when I was anxious about being online and seeing all my notifications. I also used YouTube videos and other external resources to understand concepts the course materials didn't explain well for me visually.
Q10: There were many times when I'd be reviewing downloaded materials or working through problems in a notebook, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd spend hours with my notes, creating visual summaries and diagrams, but the LMS would just show I hadn't logged in. My offline studying was actually some of my most focused work.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then download it or take screenshots of key frames to review later. I preferred studying with materials I already had rather than going back online where I'd see all the things I was behind on. It was a way to manage my anxiety while still trying to learn.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. My anxiety spiked, and I started avoiding classes and the LMS altogether. My attendance dropped significantly, and I felt like I was in too deep to catch up. The CBT sessions helped my mood somewhat, but the academic damage was already done by then.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or anxious, the LMS felt like this mountain I couldn't climb. I'd log in, see everything I needed to do, and just freeze. The workload wasn't even that unreasonable, but my mental state made it feel impossible. I'd end up doing the bare minimum or nothing at all.
Q14: All the time, honestly. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. My brain was there physically but checked out mentally. High anxiety does that to me—I go through the motions without actually absorbing anything.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important for my grade. The anxiety made starting feel impossible, and the longer I waited, the worse it got. It's like this paralysis where you know exactly what you need to do but can't make yourself do it. I'd watch the deadline approach with dread.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have these bursts of panicked activity right before something was due. My LMS usage probably looked like nothing for days, then suddenly a lot of logins and submissions. It wasn't a healthy pattern, but it was the only way I could function.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but critical feedback, even when constructive, sent my anxiety through the roof. Sometimes I'd avoid checking my grades or comments because I was scared of what I'd see. That avoidance probably hurt me more in the long run.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it assumed a level of consistent engagement I couldn't maintain. Having three online courses at once was overwhelming. The structure didn't account for students like me who struggle with anxiety and need more flexibility or check-ins. I felt like I was expected to be self-directed when I needed more support.
Q19: The visual elements of the course made engagement easier when I could focus. But the sheer volume of content and the way assignments piled up made it harder. There wasn't much flexibility for catching up once you fell behind, which created this snowball effect where I felt increasingly hopeless about recovering.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. I'd log in, watch videos, browse around, but then not submit work because I was either too anxious about it being imperfect or I'd run out of time. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just anxious scrolling and avoidance behavior.
Q21: That's kind of the opposite of my situation, but I can see how it happens. Some people are efficient—they get what they need and work offline. For me, low activity usually meant avoidance, not efficiency. But I know classmates who barely logged in and did fine because they studied from textbooks or study groups.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show what was happening in your head. They can't measure anxiety, understanding, or genuine engagement. They miss all the offline studying, the mental struggles, and the difference between productive time and anxious scrolling.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about the material and wanting to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's when you're mentally present and curious, not just checking boxes. For me, real engagement happened in brief moments when my anxiety was low enough to actually think clearly and enjoy learning.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I actually wanted to succeed or how hard I was fighting against my own anxiety. It can't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials or the mental energy I expended just trying to log in. My engagement was real, even if my performance and activity logs don't reflect it.
